rationale: This structure moves beyond the five traditional, overly broad branches of chemistry (organic, inorganic, etc.) towards a more functional, topic-based division that reflects modern research. Key, cross-disciplinary fields like 'Catalysis' and 'Chemical Biology' are elevated to top-level status, as this is how many researchers identify. We deliberately combined 'Organometallic Chemistry' with 'Catalysis' due to their immense overlap in literature and practice. The count of 12 provides sufficient granularity for specialists without overwhelming a generalist, capturing major fields like synthesis and analysis alongside growing areas like supramolecular and green chemistry.

subspecialties.name: Organic Synthesis
subspecialties.tagline: The art and science of constructing carbon-based molecules.
covers: Development of new synthetic methods and reactions
explicitly_excludes: Catalyst design and mechanism (→ Organometallic Chemistry & Catalysis)
Synthesis for drug discovery (→ Medicinal Chemistry)

subspecialties.name: Organometallic Chemistry & Catalysis
subspecialties.tagline: Designing metal-based catalysts for efficient chemical transformations.

subspecialties.name: Analytical Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Developing methods to detect, identify, and quantify chemical substances.
explicitly_excludes: Fundamental spectroscopy and dynamics (→ Physical Chemistry)

subspecialties.name: Chemical Biology
subspecialties.tagline: Using small molecules to probe and control biological processes.

subspecialties.name: Medicinal Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Designing and synthesizing new molecules for therapeutic use.
explicitly_excludes: Biological mechanism of action studies (→ Biology)

subspecialties.name: Polymer Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Creating and studying macromolecules for new materials and functions.
explicitly_excludes: Bulk material properties and device fabrication (→ Materials Science)
Biopolymers in a purely biological context (→ Biology)

subspecialties.name: Computational & Theoretical Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Modeling molecules, materials, and reactions at the atomic level.

subspecialties.name: Physical Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Investigating the fundamental physical principles of chemical systems.
explicitly_excludes: Purely computational studies (→ Computational & Theoretical Chemistry)
Analytical applications of spectroscopy (→ Analytical Chemistry)

subspecialties.name: Inorganic Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Exploring the synthesis, structure, and reactivity of non-carbon compounds.

subspecialties.name: Supramolecular Chemistry & Self-Assembly
subspecialties.tagline: Designing complex systems held together by non-covalent forces.

subspecialties.name: Electrochemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Exploring the interface of electricity and chemical reactions.
explicitly_excludes: Bulk electronic properties of materials (→ Materials Science)
Standard redox reactions in solution (→ Organic Synthesis, Inorganic Chemistry)

subspecialties.name: Environmental & Green Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Designing sustainable chemistry and studying chemicals in the environment.
explicitly_excludes: Catalysis not focused on green metrics (→ Organometallic Chemistry & Catalysis)

uncovered_concerns: The line between 'Chemical Biology' (in Chemistry) and 'Biochemistry' (in Biology) is inherently fuzzy and will require careful paper-level classification.
Materials Chemistry is a major area of overlap; the distinction relies on focusing on molecular synthesis (Chemistry) vs. bulk/device properties (Materials Science).
Photochemistry was considered as a separate category but was ultimately absorbed into Physical, Organic, and Catalysis as it is more of a cross-cutting technique than a standalone field.
